title: PgBouncer transaction pooling and prepared statements
content: Database connection pooling notes. In PgBouncer transaction pooling mode a server connection is returned to the pool at the end of each transaction, so thousands of clients share a few backends. The catch: session-scoped features break — server-side prepared statements, session advisory locks, LISTEN/NOTIFY, and SET commands do not survive across transactions. Disable prepared statements in the driver or use protocol-level extended query with care.

title: PgBouncer session pooling for long-lived connections
content: Database connection pooling notes. PgBouncer session pooling mode assigns a server connection to a client for the whole duration of the client connection, releasing it only on disconnect. This preserves session state — prepared statements, temporary tables, advisory locks, and SET all work — but it gives almost no multiplexing benefit, since idle clients still hold a backend. Use it when you need full session semantics and have a bounded client count.

title: Odyssey pooler versus PgBouncer at scale
content: Database connection pooling notes. Odyssey is a multithreaded Postgres pooler; unlike single-process PgBouncer it spreads client connections across worker threads, which matters on many-core hosts under heavy connection churn. Both support transaction pooling; Odyssey adds per-route pools and better TLS throughput. Choose Odyssey when a single PgBouncer process becomes CPU-bound on one core.

title: JWT access token short expiry
content: Auth design notes. The access token is a signed JWT the client sends on every request; keep its lifetime short (5 to 15 minutes) so a leaked token is useful only briefly. Because it is stateless the server cannot revoke it before expiry — that is the trade-off for skipping a database lookup per request. Keep claims minimal: subject, scopes, issued-at, and expiry.

title: JWT refresh token rotation
content: Auth design notes. The refresh token is long-lived and used only to mint new access tokens. Rotate it on every use: issue a new refresh token and invalidate the old one, so a stolen-and-replayed refresh token is detected when the legitimate client presents the now-revoked value. Store a per-family identifier server-side so an entire compromised chain can be revoked at once.

title: Where to store JWTs in the browser
content: Auth design notes. Storing a JWT in localStorage exposes it to any XSS on the page; storing it in a cookie exposes it to CSRF unless you set SameSite. The common compromise is an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie for the refresh token plus an in-memory access token that never touches persistent storage, so an XSS payload cannot exfiltrate the long-lived credential.

title: Marathon taper week
content: Training log notes. The taper is the final 2 to 3 weeks before a marathon: cut weekly volume by 40 to 60 percent while keeping some race-pace intensity, so the legs arrive fresh without losing sharpness. Do not add new workouts during the taper; the fitness is already banked. Expect phantom tiredness and mild anxiety — that is normal and not a sign of lost form.

title: Marathon long-run pacing
content: Training log notes. The weekly long run builds fatigue resistance; run it 60 to 90 seconds per mile slower than goal marathon pace so it develops aerobic endurance rather than becoming a race. Cap it around 2.5 to 3 hours regardless of distance, since time on feet past that yields diminishing returns and a steep recovery cost. Practice race fueling on these runs.

title: Marathon carbohydrate loading
content: Training log notes. Carbohydrate loading fills muscle glycogen for the final 2 to 3 days before a marathon: raise carbohydrate intake to roughly 8 to 10 grams per kilogram of body weight while tapering training so the fuel is stored, not burned. It is a fueling protocol, not a taper and not a pacing plan — the weight gain you see is mostly the water bound to stored glycogen.

title: Kubernetes liveness probe
content: Cluster operations notes. A liveness probe answers 'is this container wedged?' — when it fails, the kubelet KILLS and restarts the container. Use it only for unrecoverable states like a deadlocked event loop; a liveness probe that checks a downstream dependency will restart-loop your pod during an outage that a restart cannot fix.

title: Kubernetes readiness probe
content: Cluster operations notes. A readiness probe answers 'can this container serve traffic right now?' — when it fails, the pod is REMOVED from Service endpoints but NOT restarted. Use it for warm-up, cache priming, or a temporarily unavailable dependency, so traffic drains away and returns without killing the process.

title: Kubernetes startup probe
content: Cluster operations notes. A startup probe answers 'has this slow-booting container finished starting?' — while it is still failing, the kubelet suspends the liveness and readiness probes so a long boot is not mistaken for a wedge and killed. Use it for legacy apps with a long initialization, then let liveness take over once startup succeeds.
